description: Cycads (Cycas revoluta — commonly sold as Sago Palm — and related Zamia species) are among the most hepatotoxic plants known to veterinary medicine. They contain cycasin, a methylazoxymethanol glycoside that is metabolised by gut bacteria into a potent liver toxin, as well as beta-methylamino-L-alanine (BMAA), a neurotoxic amino acid. All parts of the plant are toxic, but the seeds carry the highest cycasin concentration. Ingestion of even a small number of seeds can cause acute, irreversible liver failure, and the survival rate is low even with aggressive veterinary treatment.
name: Cycad
safetyNotes: Symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea (often with blood), severe lethargy, jaundice (yellow skin/eyes/gums), abdominal pain, neurological signs including seizures; liver failure develops within 24–72 hours.
Seeds are the greatest danger: One or two seeds can be fatal. The entire plant — leaves, roots, and seeds — contains toxic compounds.
Action: This is a life-threatening emergency. Seek immediate veterinary care if any part of the plant has been ingested — do not wait for symptoms.

symptoms.name: Vomiting, Diarrhea, and Abdominal Pain
symptoms.onset: Rapid; typically within 15 minutes to 4 hours of ingestion.

symptoms.name: Acute Liver Failure, Jaundice (Icterus)
symptoms.onset: Delayed; typically develops within 48 to 72 hours.
